data.title: BlackRock Target Allocation ETF Model Portfolios Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Diversified portfolios that adapt as markets change

Investors experienced whiplash over the quarter as unpredictable trade policy and geopolitical uncertainty unleashed extraordinary market volatility and tested resolve across asset classes. The U.S. Administration's "Liberation Day" reciprocal tariffs in April initially stunned investors. Stocks spiraled lower and yields surged higher before a sharp reversal ensued as markets adjusted to moderating rhetoric from the White House and more optimistic comments about potential U.S.- China trade talks. Despite excessive economic uncertainty and a contraction in Q1 GDP, the labor market's surprising resilience provided a crucial counterbalance to growing recession fears. While inflation continued to show signs of softening, unrealized but lingering tariff-driven price pressures vexed the Federal Reserve enough to maintain a restrictive stance on monetary policy.

Meanwhile, geopolitical tensions escalated in June as conflict between Israel and Iran sparked volatility but had a relatively muted impact on markets. Ultimately, a combination of easing tariff rhetoric and robust earnings results supported positive returns for most risk assets over the quarter, with large cap tech stocks driving equity market returns. In bond markets, U.S. Treasury yields steepened as market participants digested the potential of widening U.S. fiscal deficits. Globally, European government bonds outperformed the U.S. with the European Central Bank (ECB) delivering two rate cuts over the period. Gold and bitcoin continued to climb as investors sought exposure to uncorrelated assets amid heightened volatility.

Notable portfolio changes

Asset class views

As of May 20th, 2025

Equities vs. fixed income Positive We trim our equities overweight from 3% to 1%, seeking totake advantage of the recent rally to opportunisticallyrecalibrate risk while maintaining a modest risk-on tilt.
Equities
U.S. Equities Positive We rotate broad U.S. equity exposure toward our highest-conviction ideas, emphasizing mega cap and AI companieswith resilient balance sheets and potential long-termstructural growth drivers, while also increasing exposure toactively managed strategies that can more tactically exploitdispersion and volatility through dynamic theme rotation.
Non-U.S. Developed Equities Negative We Increase exposure to value-oriented internationaldeveloped market stocks, seeking to moderate overallregional bets and rebalance our growth/value barbell, butstill maintain a small net underweight position relative toU.S. equities.
Emerging Market Equities Neutral We maintain a neutral view on emerging markets (EM)stocks and reduce bets against Chinese equities specifically,seeking to mitigate exposure to potential aggressiveChinese government stimulus and increasinglyunpredictable tariff headline volatility.
Factors Positive We maintain a preference for momentum and the largest andhighest-quality stocks and a more balanced value/growthbias across regions, while increasing exposure to our highest-conviction complement of active manager factor and themerotation strategies.
Fixed income
U.S. Treasuries Neutral We are generally neutral on U.S. treasuries with a modestoverweight to duration and an added preference for short-term inflation protection, seeking to hedge potential near-term tariff-driven inflation surprises while building inresilience against possible slower growth and morepronounced disinflation later in the year
U.S. investment grade credit Negative We maintain an underweight to U.S. investment grade (IG)credit due to less attractive relative yields and tight spreads,instead preferring more international IG bond exposures forits potential lower volatility, positive carry, anddiversification benefits.
High yield credit Positive We continue to hold a slight overweight to U.S. high yieldbonds, but less so than in the past, with a preference forcorporate issuers that screen well on quality and valuationmetrics.
Emerging market bonds (USD) Neutral We maintain benchmark exposure to emerging marketgovernment bonds due to elevated relative yields andhistorically attractive performance during periods ofsoftening global monetary policy and slowing inflation.
This material represents an assessment of the market environment as of the date indicated; is subject to change; and is not intended to be a forecast of future events or a guarantee of future results. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Portfolio performance

All models outperformed their benchmarks over with quarter, with equity-heavy models showing the strongest total returns. Our recent May trade to increase exposure to active strategies with a focus on Al themes was a leading contributor to active, along with our preference for broader tech and mega cap growth stocks. Exposure to U.S. factor rotation and momentum strategies also meaningfully contributed to active and total returns.

Within equities, quality and value stocks were the underperformers, only relative as market participants rotated toward more growth-oriented exposures. In bond-heavy strategies, the leading contributors to active return included convertible bonds, emerging market debt, and high-yield bonds. Gold exposure also added positively to performance. longer-duration U.S. Conversely, Treasuries detracted from both total and active returns, as yields steepened over the quarter.

Largest contributors

  • U.S. growth factor stocks
  • Technology equities

Largest detractors

  • Value and quality factor stocks
  • Longer-duration U.S. Treasuries

data.title: Cintas: A Quiet Leader That Continues To Grow
data.description:
Cintas Corp. (NASDAQ:CTAS) is a leading provider of uniform rental services and workplace solutions, serving over one million businesses across North America. Operating in the business services industry, more specifically in uniform rental, facility services, fire protection, and first aid & safety, CTAS occupies a niche that combines recurring revenue, operational efficiency, and broad sector exposure. Its customers range from small businesses to Fortune 500 companies, spanning industries such as healthcare, manufacturing, hospitality, and education.

CTAS has a market cap of $80.93 billion and offers a 0.90% (FWD) dividend yield that has been increasing of late. The stock is down almost 12% from its high in June and is 7.5% lower in the last month alone. With earnings here for fiscal year 2026 Q1, let's see the numbers and get a feel from management moving forward.

Some costs have risen for CTAS, which could cut into bottom-line numbers, but I am bullish on the stock for the long run and rate CTAS a buy. They have been extremely consistent with improvements in sales, earnings, and margins. Internal operation decisions have made way for success even amidst a few headwinds, and they are a clear leader in their industry.

Q1 Report and Guidance

CTAS reported $2.72 billion in total revenue for the quarter, slightly beating expectations and up 8.7% from last year. Adjusted EPS came in at $1.20, also marginally exceeding expectations but keeping pace with their continued consistency with growth regardless of market conditions.

The consistency with CTAS has been remarkable. Revenue has slightly increased or remained flat in every quarter over at least the last 5 years and missed expectations just one time. Total revenue is up almost 50% since 2021. Earnings have not risen by each quarter but have with each year and have also beat expectations each of the last 20-plus quarters. From 2021 to 2025, adjusted earnings for the year were reported as $2.50, $2.84, $3.25, $3.79, and $4.41. That’s about as steady as it gets and is a huge reason why I believe in the stock for a safe buy-and-hold play.

Management noted they are increasing their financial guidance and are moving revenue projections from $11.0-$11.15 billion to $11.06-$11.18 billion. They also mentioned they are bumping up earnings expectations slightly as well, now forecasting $4.74-$4.86 for the year.

Net income for the quarter was $491.1 million, their best quarter ever and also up almost 9% YoY. My biggest takeaway from the report is, again, the impressive consistency. Hardly ever is the company reporting stunning data or crazy new ideas, but they continue to chug along and see growth from smart strategic decisions.

Operations & Innovation

With organic growth continuing to rise, operations have been robust for CTAS. Gross margin hit an all-time high at 50.1%, as operational success continues for CTAS. Operating margin was 22.4%, and operating income for FY25 was $2.36 billion, which has doubled in the last 5 years. Operationally, CTAS is running on all cylinders, starting with its tech innovation that has been enrolled. SAP and their SmartTruck platform have led to logistical easing, as the software has developed route optimization, lowering fuel and labor costs. Delivery times have also improved by 15% with the software, helping improve efficiency and customer satisfaction, as recurring business is a huge chunk of their revenue. Input costs are rising, which I will highlight later in my risk analysis, but management has noted that manufacturing automation has significantly reduced per-unit production costs, allowing for their continued margin improvement.

Strategic Decisions

Their uniform rental and facility services are the bulk of their business, but the first aid and fire services segments both saw over 10% organic growth YoY. As safety regulations ramp up, cross-selling the two segments has led to an increased customer base on products such as AED rentals and eyewashing stations.

CTAS notes that less than 10% of their products are sole-sourced, reducing tariff impact and leading to greater supply chain success. They are unable to fully avoid tariffs on China and Mexico, but their geographic diversity helps mitigate tariffs and material costs. The firm shifted 20% of production to Southeast Asia, which resulted in fabric costs 8% lower last quarter. Strong strategic decisions have done CTAS well and have separated them from competitors. I don't anticipate the stock to move much from a fairly subdued report, but nonetheless still love this business for the long haul.

Peer Comparison

CTAS has easily outgained its peers over the last 5 years. From an array of financial perspectives, they seem to dominate their industry. Peers saw net income drop by an average of 24.65% YoY, whereas CTAS net income was 8.5% from the same quarter last year. Their net income margin is 17.53%; the next closest here is UniFirst (UNF) at 6.18%. They also have double the gross profit margin of the other three’s average. CTAS has had a cash flow growth rate of 10.9% in the last few years, which is up from the industry average of 7.1%. CTAS dominates the North American region with over 17% market share.

Risks to Thesis

One risk to CTAS is the rise in input costs. Cost of sales was 6.9% higher YoY due to increased costs of some materials as well as labor. Selling and administrative expenses also rose from an increase in employee-partner expenses. Their strategic decisions in diversifying material sourcing, with innovation and automation efforts, have helped subdue the expense escalation, but it still is a risk moving forward.

Lastly, valuation, of course, could be a red flag for CTAS. The stock has a (FWD) P/E of 41.23, compared to the sector median of 23.02. No question this is at a premium and looks hard to justify, especially for a value investor. However, CTAS had a P/E of around 38 in late 2022, when the stock then proceeded to nearly double over the next two years. CTAS is also off of its P/E high of 57 about 9 months ago.

Conclusion

For me, I am not even concerned about how the stock reacts to earnings this week for CTAS. I think this is a company that is quietly just going about their business in an industry that doesn’t get much attention. Institutions have taken note, with 57 hedge funds invested in the stock. I also only mentioned organic growth, and CTAS acquired business worth $232.9 million in FY2025. They are a well-oiled machine that has made efficient business decisions, beating on earnings again. Buy and hold CTAS.

data.title: Fastly: An Attractive Turnaround Story
data.description:
In 2020, Fastly (NYSE:FSLY) was one of the meme stocks, with its market cap reaching more than $12 billion at its peak. Now, about five years later, FSLY has lost about 90% of its value, and the stock has stagnated for the past three years.

While most people have moved on from FSLY, the company has actually been quietly improving under the hood. In the latest quarter, Fastly achieved double-digit revenue growth and shared encouraging updates about the business. The company also raised its guidance and now expects to return to positive annual free cash flow. With these fundamental improvements and the stock still consolidating around the lows, I believe Fastly might become a rewarding turnaround story. Therefore, I initiate FSLY with a Buy rating.

Fastly's business looks increasingly attractive, especially its security and observability portfolio

Fastly does not have a dominant market share in the CDN sector, as companies like Cloudflare (NET) and Amazon (AMZN) account for almost 70% of the market. Still, Fastly's customer portfolio includes many big names that are likely to grow their businesses long-term, such as Opera (OPRA), fuboTV (FUBO), Duolingo (DUOL), Squarespace, and more. Fastly has also been able to attract new customers quite successfully in recent quarters. In Q2 this year, Fastly had 622 enterprise accounts, 21 more than in the same period last year. Its total customer count also grew to 3,097. I believe this strong customer base should help Fastly continue growing its revenue at double-digit rates in the coming years, with additional upside from new customer acquisitions.

The company's revenue has also become less reliant on single large customers lately, which reduces risks for Fastly thanks to diversification. Its 10 largest customers accounted for 32% of total TTM revenue in Q2 2025, down from 37% in Q2 last year. Additionally, the company claims that no single customer accounted for more than 10% of total sales.

More importantly, though, Fastly's security-focused and observability businesses look increasingly attractive. With the continuing development of AI technology, there will naturally be greater focus on cybersecurity in the near future. This is why many cybersecurity stocks, like Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW) or CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD), have surged over the past couple of years; M&A activity in the sector has also been increasing. (I explored this topic in my recent article on Okta (OKTA))

In the case of Fastly, the company has several security and observability offerings that look promising. For instance, Fastly recently released an AI Bot Management product, which provides customers with better control over how AI agents and crawlers interact with their infrastructure and content. AI crawlers are responsible for an increasingly large share of traffic, and they can be detrimental to websites since they may slow down infrastructure and steal content without consent. Therefore, I believe Fastly's bot management has a clear value proposition: it can make sure infrastructure costs remain under control and AI companies pay for content if needed.

Compared with the competition, Fastly had not experienced a significant increase in security-driven demand until recent quarters. However, with Q2 numbers, the situation seems to have started to turn around. In the quarter, Fastly's Security revenue jumped 15% year-over-year to $29.3 million, compared to just 10% growth in the Network Services sales. Fastly's Compute and Observability revenue also surged by 60% year-over-year, even though it was still a somewhat negligible $4.5 million. If this trend continues or even accelerates, Fastly should see a significant increase in its revenue growth in the coming quarters.

Fastly's Numbers Show Clear Signs of a Turnaround

Taking a closer look at Fastly's recent quarterly report and business updates, I see several clear signs of a turnaround in the making. The most obvious one is Fastly's return to double-digit revenue growth (12.3%, to be precise) after four quarters of sub-10% increases. Here, the company's aforementioned security and observability businesses were key factors.

After the earnings release, FSLY saw substantial upward revisions to its earnings and revenue estimates, especially for 2026 and beyond. The company is now expected to generate positive non-GAAP EPS of $0.03 as early as next year, which is an important milestone. During the latest earnings call, Fastly's management also confirmed they now expect to generate positive free cash flow for this year. This reduces financial risks for investors, especially given that Fastly has a significant amount of debt, a total of $337.9 million, on its balance sheet. Fastly also had $321 million in cash and marketable securities, so there is no immediate danger to the company.

Moreover, Fastly's net retention rate (NRR) increased to 104% in Q2 from just 100% in Q1. In my view, this is one of the most important indicators for a SaaS company, as net retention higher than 100% directly leads to accelerated organic revenue growth and lower sales and marketing costs. To illustrate, NRR dropping below 100% is one of the reasons Asana (ASAN) has experienced a sharp decline in its revenue growth over the past two years, while its competitors like Atlassian (TEAM) or monday.com (MNDY) continue growing rapidly thanks to net dollar retention rates between 111% and 120%.

Finally, Fastly's remaining performance obligations (RPO) surged by 41% year-over-year in Q2, reaching $315 million. RPO represents revenue that will be recognized in the future, so it seems inevitable that FSLY's sales growth will accelerate in the coming quarters.

The Company's Low Valuation Presents an Impressive Risk/reward Opportunity

After FSLY's rapid drop from its highs and prolonged consolidation, the stock's valuation is starting to look attractive in light of the potential turnaround. Fastly now trades at a P/S of just 2.2, over 7x cheaper than Palo Alto and 20x cheaper than the closest competitor, Cloudflare. Since Fastly has also improved its cash generation in the latest quarters, its price to cash flow of 24 and forward EV/EBITDA of 30 look decent and might decline even further in the coming quarters.

Sure, Fastly has shown the lowest revenue growth among peers in recent years. However, with the low baseline, Fastly could start outperforming many competitors with its EBITDA growth in the near future.

As Fastly and the industry are likely to benefit from the expansion of AI technology, the company's low valuation leads to a solid risk/reward profile. If Fastly continues its revenue and earnings expansion, which it started in Q2, the company's stock might be revalued closer to a P/S ratio of 10. This would put the multiple closer to the pre-COVID levels, while still remaining lower than competitors. With this, FSLY has a chance to surge 5x in the coming years.

Risks and Key Takeaways

Like any turnaround story, investing in FSLY has its risks. My bullish thesis is based on the early signs of improving growth the company demonstrated in Q2, which is just one quarter of data. If FSLY fails to capitalize on the AI boom, the stock might continue trading flat. This could happen if dominant companies like Cloudflare capture all the additional market demand created by AI adoption. Additionally, as I mentioned above, Fastly has a significant amount of debt on its balance sheet. The company's financial risks might increase too quickly if Fastly fails to sustain positive free cash flow next year.

Despite these concerns, Fastly's risk/reward profile looks attractive. The stock's valuation is already depressed, especially compared to peers, so there is not much room for further price decline. At the same time, FSLY's Q2 improvements, such as double-digit revenue growth, a 41% surge in RPO, and net retention expanding above 100%, signify the start of a potential turnaround. Therefore, FSLY is a cautious Buy.

data.title: Amazon: Why It Won't Stop Winning
data.description:
Over the years, Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) (TSX:AMZN:CA) has successfully cemented its place in e-commerce. Yet, it did not stop as it tried to expand to other niches and build its own ecosystem. As a customer and investor here, there’s no going out once you’re in. This strategic business allows AMZN to maintain its strong market positioning, supported by its robust fundamentals. With that, its valuation stays rosy with technicals still demonstrating bullish signals.

Why AMZN Will Sustain Its Growth While Staying Unbeatable

The answer remains very simple as to what makes Amazon.com, Inc. a champion despite the intense competition across industries. In the retail market, Amazon continues to dominate the global market due to its wide coverage, brand popularity, and solid customer loyalty. The global e-commerce market generated a total revenue of $5.0T in 2024. Meanwhile, Amazon remains the leading platform with $790.3B in gross merchandise value with 390M monthly users in the same year. With the projected global market revenue growth of 5-10% this year, AMZN continues to outperform the market. Note that in Q2 2025, its YoY revenue from online stores alone rose by 10.9% from $55.4B to $61.5B.

With respect to its key peers like Alibaba (BABA), Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT), eBay (EBAY), and Shopify (SHOP), AMZN remains the alpha of the pack. SHOP appears to be very rosy with its robust revenue and EBITDA growth despite being relatively new. Yet, AMZN proves to be unparalleled with its sustained and well-balanced growth and profitability. Note that AMZN is already a mature company, so having double-digit revenue growth means it continues to capture new growth prospects and demand despite its already huge global presence. AMZN can still hardly penetrate more growth opportunities in China because of predatory competition there. Not to mention that BABA dominates it. However, its strategic business model will allow it to enjoy some revenues there.

1. AMZN's strategic business model will also remain its core growth driver. While it mostly operates in the online space, it also has some physical stores, which I think is prudent and attractive. WMT and TGT also have their own online platform, but they’re mostly selling their own items. AMZN sells its own items but also allows a multitude of small online stores or sellers to register on its platform. With that, AMZN enjoys demand both from buyers and sellers. It also limits competition with smaller businesses, especially those with a limited capacity to establish their own website and networks.

2. Amazon's capitalization on vertical integration through its owned trucks, planes, and sorting centers will allow it cover global demand with more controlled costs and expenses. This allows it to integrate its retail business with logistics and distribution. I believe this will continue to work for AMZN as it allows it to control its costs and expenses instead of relying on third-party parcel carriers or couriers like FedEx (FDX) and United Parcel Services (UPS). It will only have to think of wages for drivers and fuel expenses. And I think it will have more pricing power or at least lower cost sensitivity. Why? Oil prices remain weak, which means lower fuel prices. Until now, WTI Crude moves at $60-$65 per barrel. Despite the recent Israel-Qatar tension, the price may stay at sub-$70 levels due to the strategic oil supply hike by OPEC. Recently, it made a huge hike of 548,000 barrels per day. Next month, it will be 137,000 bpd. This will help offset the impact of geopolitical tensions to keep prices stable. As a result, it may be easy for AMZN to adjust its pricing to boost demand without sacrificing profits.

If it relies on its competitors, it may face higher pricing and cost pressures, especially now that inflation remains stubborn. And since it dominates the e-commerce market, it continues to outcompete these two US giants and maintain its huge international market presence. Considering this, it’s not surprising that AMZN may still beat them in growth and profitability.

3. AMZN has already built its own ecosystem, which will maintain its competitive advantage. With its Amazon Prime, AMZN may ensure a solid customer base. Perks and exclusive discounts ranging from shopping to streaming are offered here. With that, users can shop while enjoying series and movies exclusive to Amazon Prime. This is another thing that its retail and e-commerce competitors can’t offer. And I think this will be a forever edge for AMZN. Why? The entry barrier is already high here. Aside from Netflix (NFLX), Disney+ (DIS), Apple TV+ (AAPL), and HBO Max, there are also a lot of smaller streaming platforms across the globe. The thing is, these streaming giants, including Amazon Prime, continue to dominate the market.

Now, Amazon Prime has 240M subscribers globally, with over 70% coming from the US. More interestingly, it currently holds 22% of the total market share in the US. In my view, this strategy will continue to work for AMZN because 65% of Amazon shoppers are prime members. In addition, 64% of all online shoppers have Amazon Prime.

It is also important to note that Amazon Prime does not just enter the streaming industry for the sake of expanding. In fact, it has series and/or films that have garnered viewers recently. One perfect example is Reacher, which just ended its Season 3 last March 2025. It became the most watched series in the US with 217.5M views and 38M viewers. More recently, Ballard ranked fifth with 141.6M views and 26.3M viewers. So, it's not surprising that Amazon Prime has the second-largest of percentage of viewers in the US. This makes it risky and capital-intensive for other big retail and e-commerce competitors to emulate its strategy.

4. Its cloud computing or AWS will continue to propel its revenue growth. AWS may help Amazon maintain its predatory pricing strategies since this helps automatically adjust pricing versus demand and inventory. More time saved with less errors means more efficiency and productivity. Also, AMZN offers this technology to third-party users or renters. So, AMZN takes advantage of it to maintain a huge competitive advantage while enjoying a consistent and expanding revenue stream.

Considering this, we should note its robust growth in the past six quarters. After its YoY revenue weakened to 17.2% in Q1 2025, it rebounded to 17.5% in Q2 2025. With a high backlog in this segment, I do believe its growth can be sustained in the second half.

After analyzing all these four aspects, one fact surfaced. AMZN has always been and will always be successful at creating and increasing value from its strategies and initiatives.

Well-Positioned Amid Challenges

Stubborn inflation and tariff woes are some challenges that the retail industry faces. Its high source reliance in China for its own inventory can face more cost pressures after the US raised tariffs there. Its sellers and resellers sourcing there may be affected. This may weaken their pricing flexibility and affect margins.

However, AMZN has some insulation from inflation and tariffs since it has already established a global network of domestic and international distribution centers and fulfillment services. This helps it reduce tariff exposure from unnecessary and repeated cross-border shipping while accelerating its last-mile delivery. Another is that AMZN maintains regionalized inventory management across other countries in North America, Europe, and even Asia-Pacific. This helps cut customs delays and repeat tariffs. This also leads to lower shipping costs. To make it simple, a product from China can be shipped directly and stored in other countries. In addition, AMZN can strategize by shipping sourced products from China to less affected countries like Singapore and Japan before exporting to the US depending on the volume.

Considering its effective business model and cost management strategies, it’s not surprising that AMZN still enjoys strong topline growth. Its total revenues reached $167.7B, up by 13.3% from $148.0B YoY. It also managed its cost sensitivity as the operating leverage decreased from 55.4% to 54.4% YoY despite the inflation uptick and tariff woes. As a result, it enjoyed a double-digit margin from 9.9% to 11.4% YoY.

Moreover, AMZN maintains robust liquidity to ensure its sustainability. Its cash level has remained stable and increased in the past 11 quarters. It also comprises nearly half of the total current assets. In addition, its debt level has already decreased by over 20% in the past three years. It also has a long maturity profile since over 90% of the total amount will not mature within this year. Cash can also cover the whole amount, so AMZN has a huge capital buffer.

Valuation

AMZN has already increased by 72% since 2020. At $231.48, AMZN may appear high at first glance. But relative to its key financial metrics like the EPS and book value per share or BVPS, valuation appears justifiable. Using P/E, the stock trades at only 35.39x, the second-cheapest multiple in five years. This is also lower than the average of 48.18x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $315.12. The same applies to P//B as it only trades at 7.39x versus the average of 9.73x. Using this, the TP will be $304.73. Both price ratios show that AMZN remains reasonably cheap today. To be more conservative, I added a 10% risk consideration, lowering P/B and P/E TPs to $274.25 and $283.61, respectively. Either way, AMZN is still attractive at its current price.

Relative to its peers, AMZN appears to be the best using EV Multiple. Its EV/Revenue of 3.78x remains reasonable and lower than the peer average of 4.22x. While its revenue growth is significantly lower than SHOP, its actual valuation appears more realistic and conservative. The same applies to EV/EBITDA. Investors are still reasonably optimistic about AMZN’s profitability with an EV/EBITDA of 18.94x. Its EBITDA Margin of 20.51% shows that AMZN generates about $0.20 for every $1 revenue. This is much better than SHOP at 14.52% or $0.15 per revenue but trades at a very high multiple of over 100x. Meanwhile, EBAY appears a bit more attractive with a multiple of 17.76x and a margin of 21.65%. Yet, Amazon still wins with respect to EBITDA growth. Hence, AMZN is the most well-balanced and reasonably valued with respect to growth and profitability.

Moreover, AMZN will still be attractive even if it uses EV in a forward-looking approach. For its Q3 2025 estimates, revenue is expected to be between $174.0B and $179.5B. So, the new revenue TTM will be $684B and $689B. With that, the EV/Revenue Guidance high and low are 3.69x and 3.71x. The midpoint will be 3.70x. Using the current multiple, EV/Revenue FWD appears to have already priced in the guidance at a more conservative level of 3.58x. Even if we get the midpoint of TTM and FWD of 3.68x, AMZN’s valuation still appears reasonable today.

Technicals

AMZN maintains bullish momentum as the price keeps increasing with new highs and lows. Its recent dip to $212 was still above a minor support level of $208. This means that bullish signals still hold today. This is supported by the increasing trend of the 50-day and 200-day SMAs below the price trend. The uptrend of the 50-day SMA shows that recent market sentiments are still optimistic.

Meanwhile, MACD is still within positive territory, which means that buying volume still outnumbers selling volume. However, investors may have to be a bit more cautious as another dip may take place. The MACD line just crossed below the signal line, while the histogram moves within negative territory. This means that selling volume is increasing. Lastly, RSI shows some early bearish divergence signals. The momentum is still bullish, but some weakness is observed. Profit-takers are on the move despite the stock not being overbought yet. It is also worth noting that the most recent high at $238 did not break above the $242 resistance. On a lighter note, we can see that the stock still respects the Fibonacci Retracement. Considering its trend, the price may settle either at 0.618 or 0.786 before momentum picks up again. This will open new buying opportunities for investors.

Conclusion

Amazon.com, Inc. is unbeatable with its strategic business model that creates its own ecosystem for consumers. This is supported by its improved efficiency and robust fundamentals despite its global market coverage. Its current valuation makes it even more attractive with rosy growth prospects and upside potential. Technicals are still in sync with sustained bullish momentum, but investors may have to be a bit cautious today as selling volume starts to increase again. After weighing its fundamental strengths, growth opportunities, attractive valuation, and technical risks, I am giving this stock a buy rating.

data.title: QDVO To TSPY: QDVO's More Flexible Approach Is Important
data.description:
There are a few times when discussing investing that flexibility is a negative. Sometimes in life, like with investing, avoiding mistakes is as important as taking advantage of timely opportunities. While many investments perform well during good times, the true test of a financial choice is when that decision faces more adverse conditions.

Covered-call funds have increasingly become more common investments, and these investment options have evolved significantly in just the last 10 years. While traditional options-based funds simply sold calls against the entirety of what are usually index-based equity positions, these investments are now using more diverse holdings and varied options strategies to offer a better balance between income and total returns.

Two of the newer covered-call funds that use a more nuanced approach are the Amplify CWP Growth and Income ETF (NYSEARCA:QDVO) and the TappAlpha SPY Growth and Daily Income ETF (NASDAQ:TSPY). QDVO does not track an index, but the fund's holdings are similar to the Nasdaq-100 index, while TSPY tracks the S&P 500 (SPY).

QDVO has offered investors total returns of 31.62 percent since the fund's inception date in August of 2024, while the TSPY has offered investors total returns of 19.97 percent during this same time period.

I last wrote about QDVO in August of 2025, and I rated the fund a buy. Today, I am going to compare the Amplify CWP Growth and Income ETF to the TappAlpha SPY Growth and Daily Income ETF. While TSPY and QDVO both offer investors a solid balance between consistent income and impressive total returns, the more tactical approach that this Amplify investment uses to establish the fund's holdings and the more nuanced options-based approach should lead this investment to outperform its peers for a variety of reasons. Even though both funds should have appeal for many income investors, QDVO should offer more value for most individuals.

QDVO HAS an expense ratio of .55 percent, a trailing yield of 8.63 percent, and nearly $298.5 million in assets under management. The fund is invested 43.37 percent in technology, 20.97 percent in communication, 15.23 percent in consumer cyclicals, 5.80 percent in financials, 5.58 percent in consumer defensives, 3.46 percent in industrials, 2.85 percent in health care, 1.72 percent in basic materials, and 1.02 percent in real estate. The ETF's four largest holdings are NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), and Alphabet (GOOG). These positions make up nearly 36 percent of the investment and are similar to the Nasdaq-100 composite. QDVO's selections are also large-cap companies that come from the Russell 1000 index.

TSPY holds and tracks the S&P 500 by investing in SPY.

QDVO's selection process for the fund's portfolio is much more nuanced than TSPY, an investment that simply tracks the S&P 500. This ETF selects 20-40 companies based on these investments' earnings, cash flow, and recent performance. QDVO also rebalances and reconstitutes at the fund managers' discretion.

QDVO's approach to selling options is also more tactical than TSPY, which just sells daily index calls against the composite index. While TSPY does adjust the strike price and amount of daily index calls sold for market conditions while leaving the fund's exposure uncovered at night, QDVO selectively chooses which individual positions to sell options against. QDVO can take advantage of heightened levels of volatility in individual holdings, while TSPY's approach is limited to writing options against the S&P 500 index.

TSPY's strategy is based on empirical data showing that almost all of the gains in the S&P 500 (SPY) have occurred during overnight and pre-market hours in the morning since 1993.

Still, while TSPY's approach has worked reasonably well, this ETF also limits the fund's options strategy to sell daily 0-day-to-expiration options on an index, while QDVO sells options of different durations and at various strike prices on individual equities against positions the fund specifically selects. Both funds have paid out substantial income over the last year. While TSPY has paid out noticeably more income than QDVO over the last year, the options strategy that TSPY uses to pay out these distributions has significantly limited this investment's overall gains. TSPY's trailing 12-month yield is 13.50 percent, while QDVO's trailing 12-month yield is 8.72 percent.

QDVO's has paid out substantive income, this fund's payouts have been in a much wider range than TSPY's distributions because this ETF uses a variety of different options strategies.

QDVO's payouts over the last year have ranged from $.14 a share to $.28 a share, while TSPY's payouts have been in a much tighter range between .27 a share and .29 a share over the same time period.

TSPY's more conservative strategy of selling daily options has limited the fund's ability to capture the upside of the S&P 500, but this approach has produced more income. QDVO has offered investors much better overall returns, though both because the ETF has selected better-performing stocks and also because this investment has used more nuanced options strategies that include covering less of the fund's portfolio.

QDVO also notably outperformed TSPY during the March sell-off in 2025 by nearly 3 percent, mostly because of the fund's better selection of companies.

All investment theses have risks, and neither QDVO nor TSPY has a lot of data on them because each fund's inception date was nearly a year ago. Still, the covered-call funds that use more flexible approaches, such as the GPIX and GPIQ, have outperformed most of their peers for several years, and QDVO uses a similar and more nuanced approach to these Goldman funds that frequently leaves more of the portfolio uncovered. Still, QDVO's much more flexible approach also requires investors to trust the fund's manager, who actively runs the ETF, while TSPY offers a more consistent framework for those whose primary focus is predictable income, which some investors may find more appealing.

There has been a lot of evolution in covered call funds over the last decade, as well as the last five years, and most of the new options-based investment options have added levels of flexibility built into these ETFs' approaches to writing options contracts. QDVO has likely modeled the fund's approach after some of the more successful covered call funds that use a more nuanced strategy, and this ETF should continue to offer investors a better balance between income and total returns than TSPY.

data.title: Amer Sports: Boycott Issue And Insider Selling Overwhelm The Near-Term Bull Case
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Amer Sports (NYSE:AS) was a hold rating because the outlook was quite uncertain previously, weighed down by tariff risks and China exposure. The valuation was also not cheap at 44x forward PE. I reiterate my hold rating. The investor day did reinforce the structural growth story behind Arc'teryx and Salomon, but near-term risks are very real. The Tibet-related backlash in China and fresh insider selling have meaningfully clouded sentiment. While the multi-year runway remains intact, the stock's current valuation leaves little margin for error, keeping me cautious for now.

Structural Growth Story Gains Clarity

A recent investor day provided me with a much clearer view of the multi-year growth runway ahead. The crux of the narrative remains with Arc'teryx, and management has set out a target to achieve $5 billion in revenue by 2030 for this brand. I see a real shot for AS in achieving this if it can successfully close the awareness gap in key Western markets. For reference, unaided brand awareness today is just 8% in the US and 7% in EMEA, far below the ~25% level in the home market (Canada). The debate is whether AS can close this gap, and I think their management has a strategy in place that could work. The idea is to roughly double Arc'teryx's global store footprint from ~168 locations in 2025 to ~300 by 2030, with a longer-term target of 500-600. Besides the obvious fact that these stores act as distribution, these stores will also act as "brand billboards" that help drive awareness.

I believe this strategy could work because the physical stores are designed to create a "halo" effect that drives more sales in a new market, and not just the sales within that store's four walls. The increased visibility and local presence should also funnel consumers to the e-commerce business, and with higher consumer mindshare and demand, it should also lead to a lift in demand from wholesale partners in the region as well. Therefore, each new store should be seen as a lever that drives demand across multiple channels.

Importantly, this is no longer just an Arc'teryx growth story. The Outdoor Performance segment, mainly driven by the Salomon brand, has also seen accelerating momentum-35% y/y growth in 2Q25 (vs. 25% in 1Q25 and 13% in 4Q24). Like the Arc'teryx brand, there are structural levers here that AS can rely on to sustain this momentum.

Firstly, in Europe, AS has reset its sales structure and cleaned up its wholesale distribution. Basically, Salomon exited relationships with some wholesale partners that are similar and are focusing on a small set of key partners that can improve Salomon's brand presence. By working with such retailers, Salomon ensures its technical products are presented with the right assortment, storytelling, and visibility. This approach prevents the brand from being diluted alongside mass competitors (imagine buying a Salomon sport shoe that is placed alongside casual sports shoes). Importantly, working with these important partners would increase the product's creditability, which should help in attracting new consumers. The second lever is the expansion into "sports style." Salomon has certainly found success here with its flagship XT-6 franchise, which first gained traction in Asia before being successfully introduced into Europe. The strategic implication here is that it has helped Salomon to broaden its reach, attracting a younger consumer and, most importantly, women (who now account for 50-55% of the consumer base for these styles).

Intensifying Headwinds Cloud the Outlook

However, I am also getting more worried about the external headwinds coming at AS. Especially the risk around AS's heavy reliance on China. I described this as a double-edged sword previously, and unfortunately, that edge has become sharper (negative towards AS). The controversy around an Arc'teryx-sponsored event in Tibet has triggered a wave of social media backlash over environmental concerns and has drawn scrutiny from Chinese authorities. The situation is a lot worse than you can imagine, as there are even cases of consumers calling to boycott the brand. History has shown that such boycott episodes can translate into very real revenue hits, even if short-lived. Past examples involving Nike, Adidas, and Li Ning illustrate the potential severity. This is no small risk. China is not just another market; it is arguably Arc'teryx's most profitable market, with stores there generating ~$10 million in annual revenue per unit and four-wall EBITDA margins of ~50% (per the investor day presentation). This makes management's already revised growth in China (to moderate to a low-double-digit CAGR) risky since this adds another layer of uncertainty that could cause Arc'teryx to miss guidance. Depending on how bad this becomes, we could really see a "demand shock" in the near-term, causing AS to print really bad headline numbers.

This comes at a time when insiders are selling. The recent 13/D filing showed that Anamered Investments Inc. sold more than 4 million shares on August 25 for proceeds of ~$159.7 million. Although this is a small amount compared to the entire position, it still impacts sentiment, in my view.

Valuation

Overall, the combination of this boycott issue and consumer spending risk in China, alongside insider selling, makes the current valuation feel increasingly stretched. While multiples have come down to ~33x forward PE (from 44x), it is still near the historical average. I may be overly bearish on AS, but looking at consensus estimates, it doesn't seem like any potential demand shock from China is being reflected. Revenue growth is still expected to be in the mid-teens moving forward, with EPS expected to grow >25% in the near term. With sentiment running this hot, the margin for error is little to none. Any stumble in China or further insider selling could easily overwhelm the near-term bull case, and we could see multiple further contraction.

Conclusion

In my view, AS is still a high-quality structural growth story, but the timing for investors has become far less favorable. The boycott risk in China threatens its most profitable market, while insider selling has added another layer of pressure to sentiment. With valuation still at the historical average and consensus estimates not fully baking in downside risk, I find the near-term setup skewed to the downside. My rating for AS is a hold, as I prefer to wait for greater clarity on China.

data.title: Eye-Popping Moves In Quantum Stocks
data.description:
Quantum computing stocks have had another parabolic run recently. There are now four "quantum" stocks with market caps in the billions, and we provide recent price charts of them below.

IonQ (IONQ) is up the least of the bunch, with a gain of 1,000% since its lows late last year. Quantum Computing (QUBT) is up the 3rd most with a gain of 1,433%, while Rigetti (RGTI) and D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) are both up more than 2,400%.

At their core, these "quantum" companies are working on the long-term promise of quantum computing: leveraging quantum mechanics to perform computations exponentially faster than today’s computers. The technology has the potential to transform industries such as cryptography, pharmaceuticals, and logistics, but the reality is that commercial viability remains decades away. Systems are still experimental, error-prone, and difficult to scale, with many breakthroughs required before they can solve real-world problems at scale. That hasn't stopped speculators from bidding up quantum stocks to ungodly valuations, though.

As shown below, IONQ is projected to do $91.3 million in sales this year, which is the most of the group. Its market cap is all the way up to $24.4 billion, however, which gives it a price-to-sales ratio of 267! And that's the smallest P/S ratio of the four stocks shown. 2025 sales estimates for RGTI are $8.2 million, while they're $24.6 million for QBTS and just $400k for QUBT.

data.title: Amazon: Big, Fast, Relentless
data.description:
This is the first time I am writing about Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) (TSX:AMZN:CA), despite having personally invested in their stock for some time. Today, I want to explain why I remain enthusiastic and keep buying Amazon’s shares, even though this company is already a giant with a market value of $2.5 trillion.

In short, Amazon is a company that quite literally never sits still. To me, it behaves more like a small growth company than the undisputed e-commerce and cloud computing leader it is. Its constant innovation and “flywheel” approach to expansion, where each new industry it penetrates benefits its existing ecosystem, are impressive. This is not a company sitting back to enjoy its success. This is a growth machine always looking for the next opportunity. And that’s why I’ll kick off my official first coverage of the company with a Strong Buy rating.

Factors Speaking for Buying AMZN

Let’s start with the obvious introduction: Amazon’s pole position in e-commerce and cloud computing. Amazon is by far the world’s largest online retailer. No other company even comes close in terms of global reach and sales. And analysts expect this scenario to continue with a CAGR of around 18.9% through 2030 for the industry. There’s no doubt that being number one in this market is a big advantage.

As for the company’s cloud computing, this area is going from strength to strength: AWS holds about 32% of the global market, giving it a comfortable lead ahead of competitors like Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud. The global cloud industry is also projected to grow at 22% CAGR through 2023, and AWS remains a clear leader.

These two businesses alone are enough to justify my bullish view; the e-commerce side provides a steady cash flow while the cloud services deliver high-margin profits. But, as I mentioned, this is not a company that sits idle, and there’s so much more to feel bullish about.

Firstly, Amazon is, of course, poised to take advantage of the AI boom. Amazon’s leading position in the cloud computing industry integrates beautifully with generative AI. The above recent chart from Goldman Sachs shows just how quickly this technology is gaining a decent share of corporate budgets. We can see that in January 2024, CIOs expected to spend just 3% of their IT budgets on generative AI within the year and 7% in the next three years. By July of that year, that latter figure had jumped to 9%. This means that companies are planning to spend more on AI than they first thought. As the world’s leading cloud provider, AWS stands to benefit from the budget allocations, and let’s not forget that corporate AI projects require computing power, and AWS has exactly the kind of power to quench their thirst.

Another bullish development is Amazon’s strategic partnership with Anthropic, a leading generative AI company. The partnership enables AWS companies to use Anthropic’s advanced AI models, boosting AWS’s appeal to developers and companies that need AI tools. It’s a move that bolsters Amazon’s position at the front of the queue when it comes to getting some of those AI budgets.

Thirdly, true to its growth mentality, Amazon is securing its future by developing its own growth drivers alongside its core businesses. These include video streaming, digital advertising, and augmented reality (AR). Let’s take a closer look at some of the more bullish drivers.

Amazon Prime Video recently secured broadcast rights to livestream the Masters golf tournament next year. Live sports are always premium content that attracts millions of viewers across the world, so these types of rights are a big money-earner and will not only retain existing Prime customers but also attract new ones. What I appreciate about this driver is that it dovetails beautifully with the company’s existing Prime ecosystem. It’s taking an existing core service and adding a differentiator. Simple yet powerful.

Amazon isn’t just stopping at golf either. It is bringing its Emmy-winning Prime Vision sports technology to European football, a move that will strengthen Prime Video subscriptions in one of the world’s biggest sports markets. The company has also signed a deal with Netflix to launch programmatic advertising, a system that automates the buying and selling of digital ads. This means new high-margin advertising revenue streams for both companies while expanding Amazon’s existing advertising business.

And here’s an interesting one: AR. Reports suggest that Amazon is developing AR glasses, which opens up a whole new market of wearable technology. AR and VR are still early-stage technologies, but the potential for growth is no doubt enormous. I like the fact that Amazon is getting in there early before the market becomes overcrowded.

All of these moves highlight Amazon’s impressive way of “flywheeling” its business—each new product or service is designed to strengthen the company’s existing range. For example. Prime Video keeps people subscribed to Prime, and Prime members generally shop more on Amazon, taking advantage of the ‘free’ shipping. Meanwhile, AWS powers the whole operation. The more each part grows, the stronger the ecosystem becomes.

Another important point here is that even though Amazon is expanding into new areas, it is also actively growing its core business by simply finding new ways to improve customer experience and penetrate new markets. Take the Whole Foods expansion to Asia as an example. While Amazon’s Whole Foods brand is already well established, it is now launching in Singapore, making a significant geographic expansion into a lucrative region that has a strong demand for organic products. Another example is Amazon’s partnership with Winn-Dixie to expand its grocery deliveries in Florida. It’s a move that strengthens Amazon’s position in the highly competitive grocery market while also creating recurring revenue streams.

Both of these examples highlight how Amazon treats even its core e-commerce business, which is technically mature, like a brand new growth business. By constantly adding new markets and services, it is constantly developing, always on the defensive.

A final bullish argument is the valuation. You’d expect it to be sky-high, but it’s not. Surprisingly, several key ratios, especially those tied to profitability, are now lower than the company’s five-year averages. Amazon’s P/E ratio and price-to-cash-flow ratio are below historical averages, comparisons that show the stock is cheap compared to its own history.

Factors Speaking Against Buying AMZN

While AWS is a market leader, rivals like Microsoft and Google are nipping at its heels. Both these companies are also cash-generating giants that invest billions of dollars in tech every year. If they succeed in catching up—and overtaking—AWS’s growth will slow.

Although Amazon’s profitability looks attractive, the company’s P/S ratio is on the high side when you compare it to past ratios. And if revenue growth slows, it could rise further, which will make the stock look more expensive and add a bit of short-term pressure.

Finally, managing a global empire that not only spans e-commerce and cloud computing but all the other developments I have mentioned is a pretty complex job that demands reliability all around. One misstep, whether in logistics, regulation, or technology, could spell setbacks for the company.

The Verdict

There is no doubt that Amazon is a unique company. Everyone knows of it because it dominates two of the world’s fastest-growing industries: e-commerce and cloud computing. Meanwhile, it is gearing itself up for other high-potential areas such as digital advertising, AR, and live sports streaming. And its partnership with Anthropic brings it firmly into the generative AI arena. Equally important is that despite its size, Amazon is a company that has no desire to act its age. It continues to pursue growth strategies like it’s a much younger company. And that’s a positive. Its expansion into Asia for its Whole Foods brand while exploring new AR hardware and boosting the Prime membership ecosystem are just some of the examples of its boundless energy.

data.title: Janus Henderson Short Term Income Managed Account Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Portfolio returned 1.47% (gross) and the Bloomberg 1-3 Year US Gov/Credit Index returned 1.27%.

Contributors/detractors

Our overweight allocation to spread risk was the primary contributor to relative outperformance, while exposure to agency mortgage-backed securities detracted.

Outlook

In our view, diversified fixed income continues to offer attractive nominal yields, coupled with the diversification benefits investors have come to expect from a core bond allocation.

Investment environment

  • The U.S. short-term fixed income market registered a positive return for the quarter, with the Bloomberg 1-3 Year U.S. Government/Credit Index rising 1.27%. Treasury yields rallied at the short end of the curve, helping to drive positive returns. Spread sectors outperformed comparable maturity U.S. Treasuries.
  • Risk markets were broadly higher, despite April's sell-off in response to the Trump administration's "Liberation Day" tariffs and an escalation in geopolitical tensions between Iran, Israel, and the U.S. in June.
  • Numerous partial trade concessions and a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs helped assuage market anxieties regarding the expected adverse effects of tariffs, resulting in a rally in spread sectors through quarter-end.
  • The labor market remained resilient, though job gains moderated, with unemployment staying low and ending at 4.1%. The Federal Reserve (Fed) took no rate actions during the quarter, while the Trump-backed tax and spending bill advanced through Congress despite concerns over the implications for U.S. debt levels.
  • The U.S. 2-year Treasury yield ended the quarter at 3.72%, relative to 3.88% at the end of March. Investment-grade corporate spreads tightened 11 basis points (BPS) to 83 bps, while high-yield spreads narrowed 57 bps to 290 bps.

Portfolio review

Our overweight allocation to diversified spread sectors contributed to relative outperformance, as spreads tightened through the quarter on easing trade tensions and a more favorable outlook.

We had increased our corporate exposure late in the first quarter, as wider spreads provided opportunities to acquire high-quality corporate bonds at attractive valuations. As corporate spreads rallied through the end of the second quarter, we rotated some of our corporate exposure into Treasuries. We maintained our overweight allocation to securitized sectors, where valuations continue to look attractive on an absolute and relative basis.

Notably, our allocation to high-yield corporates was the key contributor for the period. Exposure to collateralized loan obligations, non-agency mortgages, and commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) also contributed. An allocation to agency MBS detracted.

In terms of interest-rate risk, we remained overweight the short end of the yield curve, as this part of the curve likely will rally if the labor market or economy weakens, while being less likely to be adversely impacted by increases in term premium caused by concerns around fiscal policy or Fed independence.

With Treasury yields and short-term interest rates approximating their highest levels since 2007, we believe investors should view this as an opportunity to continue reinvesting at attractive yields – something they likely wouldn't be able to do if yields fell sharply.

We feel the portfolio remains well diversified, with exposure to an array of fixed income sectors, and we maintain a bias toward higher credit quality. We believe this approach is key to harvesting the attractive yields on offer in fixed income markets, while simultaneously managing risk and volatility.

Manager outlook

Following an initial bout of volatility after the Liberation Day announcement, trade tensions have subsided and the probabilities and magnitude of downside scenarios for the global economy have decreased. While uncertainty remains regarding the eventual outcome of Trump administration's trade and immigration policies, we believe the outlook for the U.S. economy remains upbeat. Despite mixed economic data, employment and economic growth remain resilient for now, with second-quarter gross domestic product (GDP) estimates around 2.5% to 3.0%.

Inflation has continued its gradual downward trend. While tariff-induced price increases are yet to show up in the data, we expect tariffs may more likely result in a one-time upward price adjustment than in snowballing inflation, and thus far long-term inflation expectations have remained in check. As a result, we believe the Fed is still well positioned to provide relief through rate cuts if the economy and labor markets weaken.

Credit spreads have tightened again to reflect the improved outlook, with valuations appearing less compelling on the surface. However, we continue to find opportunities for attractive risk-adjusted returns in various pockets of fixed income markets. More broadly, we believe the present valuation environment calls for an approach focused on maximizing income per unit of volatility and limiting drawdown risks. The current market, in our view, is more of a "carry" and bond pickers' market, in which investors are rewarded by clipping relatively high coupons and picking individual bonds with positively skewed risk/return expectations, as opposed to making macro bets.

Many fixed income sectors continue to offer attractive nominal yields in the mid-to-high single digits, while bonds have once again begun to exhibit their diversification benefits by way of low or negative correlation to equities and negative rate-spread correlation. Taken together, we consider the combination of diversification benefits and attractive nominal yields essential to managing portfolios through the evolving economic landscape.

At a glance

Performance

The Portfolio returned 8.18% (gross) and the 60% MSCI ACWI / 40% Bloomberg Glb Agg 3-5 Yr Index returned 8.89%.

Contributors/detractors

Positioning in the fixed income market detracted from relative performance, while overweight exposure to both U.S. and international equities contributed.

Outlook

We see positive fundamentals for financial markets, given our view that the economy will remain resilient, disinflation will continue, and recession is a low probability.

Investment environment

  • Global equity markets delivered positive returns in the second quarter, recovering from a sharp sell-off in early April after the U.S announced sweeping tariffs. Shares rebounded later in the quarter after the U.S. Trump administration suspended some of the levies.
  • U.S. stocks modestly underperformed non-U.S. stocks in U.S. dollar terms. The U.S. dollar weakened to a threeyear low against a basket of major currencies due to uncertainty around U.S. trade policy.
  • Global fixed income securities had positive returns as yields on core 10-year government bonds – including in the U.K., Germany, and Japan – generally fell. The yield on U.S. benchmark 10-year Treasuries ended the quarter relatively unchanged despite market turmoil in April.

Portfolio review

Positioning in the fixed income market detracted from relative performance. This was due, in part, to a significant underweight in unhedged international sovereigns. This underweight detracted as international bonds outperformed their U.S. counterparts in U.S. dollar terms. The portfolio's overweight allocation to U.S. fixed income also detracted on a relative basis.

We saw typically above-average attractiveness for U.S. fixed income, with typically below-average attractiveness for non-U.S. fixed income. Both exhibited elevated levels of downside risk, although the risk for U.S. fixed income declined to near-average levels by quarter-end.

Equity investments were relative contributors, aided in part by a moderate overweight in U.S. equities. The portfolio's overweight allocation to international equities also contributed.

Over the period, our forward-looking risk signals, extracted from option prices, indicated somewhat below-average attractiveness for both U.S. and non-U.S. equities. Our signals also showed typically below-average levels of downside risk, excluding a brief spike in April following the initial U.S. tariff announcements.

The portfolio's equity and fixed income exposures during the period averaged 65.31% and 32.01%, respectively.

Manager outlook

The second quarter was characterized by political "trade war" and geopolitical risk. Trade risk reached its apex on April 2, the day President Trump labeled "Liberation Day." On this day, the levels of announced reciprocal U.S. tariffs onto imports were higher than even the most feared estimates. The market response was fierce as investors priced in a higher probability to global recession. Political risk, unlike structural economic risk, can rapidly dissipate because policy can quickly change. This is especially true with an administration that acts at fast and furious speeds. This turned out to be the case with the tariff-induced fear that quickly faded as President Trump suspended the levies to allow for countries to work out their trade issues. While the damage to risk assets was short lived, the damage to the U.S. dollar remains, as the goal to neutralize the U.S. trade deficit drains dollars from offshore coffers, weakening the greenback's status as the global reserve currency.

Based on option market pricing of equity and fixed income downside and upside risks, we believe the economy will remain resilient, disinflation will continue, and recession is a low probability. Our estimates of expected left-tail risk to both U.S. and non-U.S. equities continue to trend downward, while stock attractiveness trends upward. These are all good signs. Additionally, recessions tend to hit when the consumer and small businesses are strained and their financial balance sheets are weakened. A direct measure of this potential stress that we like to look at is how U.S. regional banks are assessing their loans. Borrowers from regional banks largely represent consumers and small businesses, hence the quality of these loans provides direct insight into their health. Net charge-offs as a function of total loans for regional bank loans are well below levels seen during the 2008 global financial crisis. This finding supports a low recession probability. Charge-offs, however, are trending up, and it will be important to observe how second-quarter earnings reflect this trend.

The largest threat to our resilient growth outlook is the threat to "U.S. exceptionalism" and a movement away from American assets and services. Such a scenario could trigger an abrupt domestic slowdown and subsequent global contagion, leading to a drop in the dollar and higher inflation. To gain historical insights into the impact of movement away from U.S. assets, we analyzed the average returns of bonds and equities as a function of U.S. dollar index returns. Historical data indicate that the return on large-cap U.S. stocks does not show much of a relationship to the dollar, except when the dollar is very strong. This may coincide to periods of "flight to safety" and equity fragility. However, there is a historical relationship between interest rates and the dollar. During periods of dollar depreciation, U.S. interest rates fall, but the causality is not clear. Is the dollar weakness because the currency's carry is lower and carry investors move away from the U.S., or is it because of a shift in demand away from the dollar? The data suggest causality, in fact, runs one-way, with a falling dollar leading to a drop in U.S. interest rates.

In economic terms, the transmission may be that as the demand for U.S. assets or services falls, the dollar and the American real economy weaken, leading to accommodative monetary policy. This is a very important observation because it suggests that the weaker dollar does not ignite inflation or hamper monetary stimulus. This accommodative policy could result in U.S. rates falling and supports a rise in domestic equities.
